learning.summary: A bootable USB drive is removable installation media that a computer can start from in order to install an operating system. It is commonly used when installing or reinstalling Windows, Linux, or other operating systems, but the system firmware must support booting from USB and the boot order may need to be adjusted.
learning.explanation: A bootable USB drive contains the files needed to start an operating system installer outside of the computer's existing installed operating system. Instead of launching setup from within a working OS, the computer starts from the USB device and loads the installation environment from that media.

facts.text: A bootable USB drive is a common method for installing an operating system.
Bootable USB installation media allows a computer to start an OS installer from removable storage.
The computer firmware must support booting from USB for a bootable USB installer to work.
The boot order or one-time boot menu may need to be changed so the system starts from the USB drive instead of the internal storage device.

examples.text: A technician creates Windows installation media on a USB drive, plugs it into a computer, selects the USB device from the boot menu, and starts the Windows setup process.
A student testing Linux can boot from a prepared USB installer and install the operating system onto a target drive.

notes: Bootable USB media depends on the system's firmware settings and boot-device selection process.

examTips.text: Know that a bootable USB drive is a common OS installation method.
Remember that firmware boot support and boot order can affect whether a USB installer starts successfully.

commonMistakes.text: Assuming the computer will automatically boot from the USB drive without checking boot order or the one-time boot menu.
Confusing a normal USB storage drive with a properly prepared bootable USB installer.

scenarios.situation: A technician needs to install an operating system on a computer with a blank internal drive and chooses bootable USB installation media.
scenarios.expectedAction: Identify the correct concept or response.

scenarios.situation: A computer keeps starting the existing OS instead of the installer because the internal drive is listed before USB in the boot order.
scenarios.expectedAction: Identify the correct concept or response.

pbqIdeas.task: Given a firmware boot menu, select the USB installation device as the first boot target for an OS installation.

questionTargets: Identify bootable USB media as an OS installation method.
Explain why firmware boot order matters when using USB installation media.
Distinguish bootable installation media from ordinary removable storage.

reviewNotes: Source evidence directly supports bootable USB media as a common OS installation method.
Firmware support, boot-order behavior, one-time boot menu usage, and examples are enriched instructional details based on the Discovery Review requirement and should be human-reviewed.
Related concept boot-process.boot-order is included as a suggested link because the gap review requested a boot process or boot order prerequisite concept, but no formal prerequisite was supplied.
Promoted from authored draft as needs-review canonical content.
